Abstract

3D-printed earth materials that incorporate natural raw soils have been recently emerging due to their ecological and affordability potential. However, earth materials applications in additive manufacturing have been limited to thick mass assemblies with little to no fiber reinforcement. The addition of natural plant fibers within earth-based mixtures may advantageously increase ductility while allowing for lightweight assembly types, such as thin and perforated elements. This article presents a novel research development on natural, raw, and untreated earth-fiber compositions with maximized wheat straw fiber content for 3D-printed lightweight architectural tiling applications. Initiated with an experimental printability apparatus of a range of mix designs, a printable “light straw clay” mixture is defined through extrudability and buildability tests. Then, combining the digital craft of weaving with natural fibers for earthen lightweight artifacts, a geometric analysis explores potential super lightweight and structurally sound tessellations to allow for minimum material in the production of perforated panels. The third phase of the research included structural bending tests to assess the number of layers required for the final tile production. Finally, the resulting 3D-printed modular components were assembled to create a lightweight installation, hung and exhibited with an interplay of light and shade. By maximizing co-product vegetable fiber content within an earthen and bio-based paste, this research aims to increase the carbon storage capabilities of digital earth construction while enhancing its lightness and tensile possibilities. Learning from vernacular “recipes” of natural earth- and fiber-based construction, the developed paper-thin partition assemblage presented in this article contributes to wider possibilities of natural, nonconventional, and radically low-carbon material systems and geometries in digital fabrication.

Keywords: 3D printing, light straw clay, earth construction, natural fibers, material ductility, digital-craft design

Introduction

Profound transformations are undergoing the convergence of computational design and environmental concern, with the field of advanced manufacturing at the center of focus for architectural assemblies that can minimize material spoil while utilizing low-carbon materials. This trend showcases a strong emphasis on nonconventional and natural materials for 3D printing, utilizing readily available substances such as salt, waste, and clay,1 explored as alternatives for cement- and petroleum-based additive manufacturing. Natural fibers such as hemp and kenaf have been extensively employed as reinforcements in thermoplastic polymers.2–6 Nevertheless, the incorporation of natural fibers does not consistently enhance all mechanical properties of these biocomposites.2 On the contrary, fiber inclusion in earth-based mixtures allows for reduced shrinkage and improved mechanical strength.3 Specifically, 3D-printed earth, which utilizes raw clay-rich soils directly from the construction site, is critical for socially and environmentally just architectural design to reduce transportation, chemical treatments, excess manufacturing, and intermediary storage.7,8

Current applied design research on 3D-printed earth has been focusing on artifacts, structures, and elements using mix designs resembling a vernacular method known as cob.7 Cob is an earth mixture that contains clay-rich soil, with 2–3 wt% natural fibers. This mixture is produced in a plastic state and implemented wet to build monolithic load-bearing walls with or without formwork. The presence of straw in the cob was shown to impart a ductile failure mechanism, a quality that suggests appropriate behavior in seismic areas.9 However, due to its high mass capacity and low thermal resistivity, cob assemblies result in high mass assemblies as thick as 600 mm. While critically introducing a radically natural low-carbon mix design, digital earth using cob is still limited to massive, heavy, brittle, and thick structures that work in compression.

Vernacularly, a lightweight and insulative counterpart to cob is a material system called light straw clay (also known as light clay, straw clay, slip straw, rammed straw, and leichtlehmbau in Germany). Light straw clay is an earth-fiber mixture that serves as an insulative infill mix with fiber (usually wheat straw) dampened with clay slurry (wet clay-rich soil), to be tamped within a structural frame.10,11 With an increased fiber content, light straw clay proposes a mix design characterized by a coupled insulation and mass capacity.12 Light straw clay has a mean heat transfer coefficient of 0.531 W/m2K for a 120-mm thick wall,13 making it a compatible insulation material that meets energy code requirements. In terms of its environmental impacts, light straw clay was shown to outperform all other earth—and conventional—assemblies over its cradle-to-grave life cycle.14 Light straw clay was shown to have the best embodied and operational carbon performance due to its low extractive constituent materials as well as the juxtaposition of thermal mass and thermal resistivity that reduces both heating and cooling energy demand.14

To advance the material design research of natural earth-fiber composites for 3D printing, this study aims to maximize fiber content in 3D printable earth-based mix designs, to achieve similar ratios as light straw clay, while testing the limits of maximum lightness, ductility and bending capacity, and geometrical craft. By optimizing mix designs for 3D-printed earth while maximizing fiber content, and thus, carbon storage4 and ductility, this project links, for the first time, natural 3D-printed vegetable fiber and earth artifacts for lightweight think construction. In the long term, this project hopes to push the use of recycled and recyclable bio-based materials for interior and temporary applications, an important aspect within the built environment that further requires low-carbon responses and subsequent end-of-life strategies. As recyclable natural materials can be re-plasticized when exposed to water, they also require proper design and interior applications to be protected from weatherization.15

Background on Natural Materials in Advanced Manufacturing

Natural materials in architecture are defined as readily available, minimally processed, nontoxic, and community accessible.16 Due to their traditional nature and adaptability to various shapes and construction methods, natural building materials are often reminiscent of traditional architectural techniques. Natural materials include a range of naturally occurring substances but can be summarized into two base constituencies comprising geological products, such as earth, sand, larger aggregate, lime, and stone, and biological products, such as vegetation plants, plant-like protists such as algae, agro “waste,” and co-products within a circular economy logic. This range of substances can be combined into mix designs using construction methods that form techniques such as adobe, rammed earth, compressed earth blocks, cob, hempcrete, straw-bale construction, and light straw clay.

Historically, natural earth-fiber composites can be found in almost every region of the world. Based on knowledge transmitted from one generation to the next, earth-fiber composites have been evolving over millennia into a range of techniques to respond to bioclimatic contexts and thermal and structural requirements. Traditional cob walls, for instance, can be found in temperate climates such as Devon England, heated by hearths within load-bearing assemblies using earth and straw placed in layers on stone plinths.17 In other contexts, the Hakka round rammed-earth houses in China provide a heavy thermal mass to regulate temperature and relative humidity.18 Fiber-based vernacular architecture that makes use of fast-growing and grass-like fiber-reinforced plants is found, for instance, in Peruvian Uros’s totora houses, bound with mud mortar,19 as well as thatched-roof houses in wetland areas, from South America to Africa and Europe.20 Light straw clay, which is the focus of this work, is a fiber-based mixture coated with clay-rich soil slurry, representing another example of fibrous traditional construction materials used as infill products and valued for its insulating and lightweight properties.21 As a radically sustainable material, light straw clay was shown to be crucial to maximizing carbon neutrality in the construction of thermally resistant infill assemblies.14

Despite their environmental and social benefits, natural materials are not without their limitations. Natural materials and methods, such as cob and light straw clay, are often slower and laborious. They were vernacularly termed the “slow process.”22 The main impediments to implementing earth materials in mainstream construction are indeed their lack of technical data and labor intensity throughout the construction process.23,24 Digital fabrication may, therefore, be a critical solution to increasing the implementation of natural materials, by reducing human labor intensity and the need for formwork, while also increasing speed and material economy.

The state of the art of digital earth construction includes both material and machinery developments, from an applied and educational research scope. Applied projects include large-scale earth structures using traditional adobe recipes by studio Rael San Fratello and Muddy Robbots.1 Educational fronts include experimental earthen structural geometries, such as mud staircase, with a focus on residential construction, at The Institute for Advanced Architecture of Catalonia.25,26 Other applications, summarized in Figure 1, use adapted versions of cob mix designs. As a natural reinforcement, plant fibers have been used in 3D printing applications, mostly within a petroleum- or cement-based matrix. Most relevant to this study is LivMatS pavilion, which used flax fibers within oil-based epoxy (Hexion) and bio-based epoxy matrix (Greenpoxy), a bio-based resin that consists of 52% natural resources, robotically fabricated through coreless filament winding technology.27 Additional mixtures were developed for 3D printable natural fibers.26,28,29 For instance, Rech et al. developed printable mixtures with sawdust embedded within xanthan gum, which serves as a binder for cellulose insulation.30

Continuing this line of work, an ongoing material design research at the Columbia Natural Materials Lab has been focusing on a range of earth-fiber composites for additive manufacturing.31,32 This line of research hopes to integrate additional avenues for natural fibers in 3D printing, within an earth-based binding matrix, as illustrated in Figure 1. As part of this work, computational design methods, workflows, and tools were developed to produce architectural objects while employing ubiquitous resources, as a viable alternative to synthetic insulation, or as an amelioration of heavy-mass digital earth. Inspired by light straw clay as an alternative approach to the use of natural fibers, coated with clay slurry, this article describes an experimental apparatus and design demonstration development of 3D-printed earth-fiber tiles, from material mix recipes to printability, structural testing, and fabrication of the design demonstration.

Materials and Methods

The main goal of this study is to develop perforated lightweight and ductile earth-fiber tiles. As a mixed-method approach, the methodology combines experimental, computational, and fabrication apparatuses. As shown in Figure 2, the developed workflow includes the following steps: (1) optimizing mix designs for maximized fiber content and testing each mix design for extrudability and buildability; (2) studying geometrical patterns that allow minimum material within a panel element, specifically traditional Middle Eastern shading and fabric elements; (3) testing the structural capacity of the resulting geometries for bending, using the breaking load to assess the number of layers in the final printing application, and (4) fabricating the 3D-printed modular elements into a shape-giving form to generate a hung tessellating light installation that works in tension. As a final result, this study examines and exhibits the potential of the resulting earth-fiber tiles within 1014 Space for Ideas and Gallery in New York.

Mix-design analysis

The soil used in this study is sourced from a construction recycling quarry in Goshen, NY, and is of a dark-gray color with ∼10% clay content. The fiber used was wheat straw harvested locally from NY State, which comes in longer stalks (10–12 cm) and was then blended in the laboratory with a Vitamix blender to achieve a 1–3 mm fiber length. Additionally, for the purposes of pigmentation, 0.1 wt% of natural red clay (Newman’s red clay) was added, which is a low plasticity California iron-bearing fire-clay for pottery purposes (Fig. 3). Up to 5% food-grade polysaccharide additives (cellulose and alginate) were used to support material printability and increase viscosity and green strength.

Mixtures were first assessed with a manual syringe and were then systematically tested using a Delta WASP 40100 Clay 3D printer (WASP, Italy) in terms of their extrudability and buildability with the objective of increasing the fiber content. Experiments were conducted with a nozzle of 6 mm, and pressure was kept constant at 0.4 MPa throughout the printing with a fixed layer height of 3 mm.

Structural tests

The goal of this step was to assess the number of printed layers required to achieve the minimum usable strength of the single module with minimum material content. Selected geometries were tested in 320 × 320 mm sample panels. Structural bending tests were used to assess the breaking load according to ASTM C67/C67M by applying a loading rate of 1.25 mm/min until failure. Three heights were assessed, each comprising a 3-mm layer height. Three specimens were tested for each tile type, for a total of nine elements.

The breaking load ( p) was then calculated as the average according to the following equation (Eq 1):

p=P/w (Eq. 1)

where P is the maximum load (N) indicated by the testing machine, and w is the net width of the specimen (mm) at the plane of failure, namely 65 mm (Table 1).

Results and Design Investigation

Bespoke fibrous material—earth-fiber recipe results and reusability potential

Starting with digital cob mixture proportions observed in the literature, preliminary mix designs were tested with 54 wt% of soil, 3 wt% of fibers, and 41 wt% of water. A cylinder with a 100 mm diameter, 50 mm height, and composed of 20 consecutive layers was printed for each admixture to determine the most suitable recipe in terms of printability. By keeping the soil content fixed, the fiber content was increased until it was no longer possible to extrude the paste from the 3D printer nozzle. Further detailed in previous work by the authors32, the final light fiber clay mixture used in this study consists of the ratios as presented in Table 2.

As illustrated in Figure 3, the printed cylinder in light straw clay achieves an 80% weight decrease as opposed to the printed cob cylinder while assuring the extrudability and the buildability of the material.

Importantly, the achieved material cob, light cob, and light straw clay mix designs were proven fully recyclable and reusable. Printing experimentation was fully reused by being immersed in water for 24 h with 79 wt% water, which is the percentage of water lost while drying. Once fully hydrated and “re-plasticized,” the material was manually mixed, similar to dough baking practices, to re-obtain a 3D-printable paste. The tile demonstration in this study highlights this circular capability by being printed and reused, several times throughout the process.

Perforated lattice patterns following traditional weaving and Mashrabiya designs

The pattern form-finding in this study aimed to optimize perforation and minimum material, for a 3D-printed line-deposited order. Path lines were investigated to ensure the structural integrity of the whole element, with two printed walls being braided as shown in Figure 4. A G-code generator script in Grasshopper was used to generate the final printing file.38

The geometric exploration began with an investigation into the symbolism and patterns in traditional fabric craft, followed by an investigation into shading patterns in North African and Mediterranean contexts. Traditional woven fabrics, such as the Noche de Berberisca dresses, exhibit weaved patterns that help construct fabric shape, as a reinforcement element. The investigation continued into the Arab shading element—the Mashrabiya typology found in cities with arid climates, such as Baghdad, Damascus, Cairo, Jeddah, and Tunis.33 Contemporary precedents show great interest in expanding Mashrabiya utilization beyond its traditional use. Some examples include Al Bahar Towers’ kinetic facades by Aedas Architects in Abu Dhabi34,35 and SDU Kolding Campus by Henning Larsen Architects in Denmark.36 Both precedents push Mashrabiya’s traditional use through computational design and day-lighting optimization.37

The pattern development was continued using Hilbert curves, a type of mathematical construct that generates intricate lattice patterns through a continuous line.38 Used previously by architects such as Wacław Szpakowski in single-line labyrinthine geometries,39 the Hilbert curves offered a unique approach for a “printable” line path architecture.

The geometrical exploration developed and shown in Figure 4 concludes the rhythmic lines served as guidance for creating the final tiling pattern. A square grid of points was initially created as the basis for the lattice. The Hilbert curve was then constructed by dividing the square recursively into four smaller squares and connecting them in a specific sequence. The resulting curve visits every point in the grid precisely once, creating a continuous and nonintersecting path, as illustrated in Figure 4. The lattice was constructed by connecting the points in the grid along the path of the curve. By adjusting the spacing and thickness of the lattice elements, a wide range of patterns was created that are both visually intricate and structurally robust.

Once the geometry for a single tile was determined, a modular system was developed by cataloging a grid system that could be repeated endlessly. To create a unified geometry, additional connection attachments between individual panels were designed to form a wet connection, where the modular panels were simply brought closer together to be dried uniformly. In this logic, first, a specific connector panel characterized by protrusions and insets combined within a singular tile module was designed to minimize the quantity of material printed. Several connector details were examined, as shown in Figure 5.

Structural tests for minimum tile thickness

The tested specimens, shown in Figure 6, were characterized to assess the minimum number of layers required through breaking load testing. The printing parameters for the WASP DELTA Clay 40100 were kept constant in the G-code creator as following: the linear extrusion rate (E) was equal to 30 mm/s and the flow rate (F) was defined as 1500 mm/min.

The structural results, shown in Table 1, indicate that the addition of two layers increased the average p-value by 318% when compared with a geometry with just one printed layer. When analyzing the element’s weight, it is possible to observe that elements with three layers resulted in the addition of 45% of weight and almost improved by 199% the p-value with respect to specimens with two layers. Considering that this study aimed to minimize material in production while assuring the stability of the final result, it was decided that three layers would be used in the final printed demonstration.

Furthermore, the slightly higher maximum breaking load achieved with additional layers compared with a single layer is presumably resulting from the formation of some intra-layer bonding. Independently of the printing pattern, decreasing the layer height typically leads to enhanced cohesion and tensile properties of the printed biocomposite.2 A layer height of 3 mm, which is half the nozzle size, was therefore chosen to increase the bonding strength between adjacent layers while ensuring a good load path transfer through the assembly. Future research is needed for earth-fiber mix designs to assess the impact of the fiber alignment along the printing paths and its effect on the bending strength.

Production and final demonstration

The digital fabrication steps followed three main production steps as shown in Figure 7: 3D printing of the tiles, assembly of the modular system, and installation upon drying. The printed modules were laid on top of a curved formwork made out of repurposed formwork to test the curvature potential of the material. Once the tiles are 3D printed, they are directly molded upon the reused formwork prior to the drying point while the material is still malleable. To obtain self-connected modules with the use of the wet connection, two subsequent modules were placed within 80 mm of distance, and a third module was located on top of them when the material was still wet and adjusted manually. By doing so, the final spatial tessellating geometry was bonded in a wet state, strengthening the connectivity between the tiles over a 36-h drying period. Once dried, the final 3D geometry was easily detachable from the formwork.

The final spatial tessellating geometry detail is shown at the bottom of Figure 7, illustrating the passage of light through the artifact. The resulting demonstration, composed of solely natural materials in a regenerative perspective, allowed to parametrically control the graduation of light and shadow tracing filigree motives on the floors and walls and evoking echoes of traditional patterns.

The developed materials and tectonic system were demonstrated in an interior light installation at 1014 Space for Ideas, a gallery space in East Side Manhattan. The light straw clay 3D-printed lightweight panels were designed to create a site-specific installation, placed within the gallery’s spiral 19th-century stairwell. Five tasseling panels were fabricated from a total of 39 tiles, printed over 8 hours, and hung using a transparent thread beaded using brass clips. Composed of a total of 3.5 kg straw fiber, these modular panels were connected in a wet state to form vertical curtain walls, allowing an interplay of light and shadow, as shown in Figure 8. From heavy mass to lightweight mesh, this installation demonstrates a design apparatus of spatial lines, in a line-based deposited order, to create lightweight 3D-printed earth-fiber assemblies.

Broader Implications and Future Work

The final installation demonstration provides a proof-of-concept prototype for novel earth-fiber mixtures. Additionally, this research proposes a workflow for investigating impactful, low-carbon, and lightweight fiber and earth-based materials in digital fabrication. With respect to the state of the art where 3D-printed earth materials have been used with 2% or less fiber content, this work develops an earth-fiber printing paste with 13 wt% of wheat straw content (49% natural fiber in volume).

The results of the study show that the main parameters affecting the design, printability, and final behavior of the printed tiles relate to the length, content, and type of natural fiber used within the mix design. Overall, this study proved light straw clay’s printability and structural tensile capabilities, while respecting the vernacular lexicon and mix-design recipe. An important feature of this work is the full recyclability and reusability of the developed printing mixture. The printing experimentations shown in this article were fully reused by being immersed in water for 24 h with 79 wt% of water, “re-plasticized,” and then reprinted.

The future of low-carbon earth-fiber composites in 3D printing filaments and lightweight construction materials critically suggests further applications, advancements, and demonstrations, from interior finish materials to hybrid mass-insulation wall assemblies. Future research should include comprehensive characterization and enumeration of the mechanical, thermal, and environmental performance of a range of plant fibers within “light clay” mix designs. This line of work is currently being expanded by the authors to an array of bast, stalk, leaf, and other vegetable co-product materials that can render the proposed construction methods even more resource-efficient and bioregional.

Moreover, future developments should be carried out by keeping in mind the ultimate dimensions and shipment system for upscaled product development. To make the proposed 3D printing strategies globally applicable, the manual forms of making together with the use of local agriculture and construction geological co-products in contemporary industries is critically needed to enable the development of local digital craftsmanship. Ultimately, the combination of natural materials, both geological and biological, as well as the collaboration of human/machine fabrication process opens up important pathways to environmental, social, and cultural ancient just digital-craft future.

gold_text: Abstract 3D-printed earth materials that incorporate natural raw soils have been recently emerging due to their ecological and affordability potential. However, earth materials applications in additive manufacturing have been limited to thick mass assemblies with little to no fiber reinforcement. The addition of natural plant fibers within earth-based mixtures may advantageously increase ductility while allowing for lightweight assembly types, such as thin and perforated elements. This article presents a novel research development on natural, raw, and untreated earth-fiber compositions with maximized wheat straw fiber content for 3D-printed lightweight architectural tiling applications. Initiated with an experimental printability apparatus of a range of mix designs, a printable “light straw clay” mixture is defined through extrudability and buildability tests. Then, combining the digital craft of weaving with natural fibers for earthen lightweight artifacts, a geometric analysis explores potential super lightweight and structurally sound tessellations to allow for minimum material in the production of perforated panels. The third phase of the research included structural bending tests to assess the number of layers required for the final tile production. Finally, the resulting 3D-printed modular components were assembled to create a lightweight installation, hung and exhibited with an interplay of light and shade. By maximizing co-product vegetable fiber content within an earthen and bio-based paste, this research aims to increase the carbon storage capabilities of digital earth construction while enhancing its lightness and tensile possibilities. Learning from vernacular “recipes” of natural earth- and fiber-based construction, the developed paper-thin partition assemblage presented in this article contributes to wider possibilities of natural, nonconventional, and radically low-carbon material systems and geometries in digital fabrication. Keywords: 3D printing, light straw clay, earth construction, natural fibers, material ductility, digital-craft design Introduction Profound transformations are undergoing the convergence of computational design and environmental concern, with the field of advanced manufacturing at the center of focus for architectural assemblies that can minimize material spoil while utilizing low-carbon materials. This trend showcases a strong emphasis on nonconventional and natural materials for 3D printing, utilizing readily available substances such as salt, waste, and clay,1 explored as alternatives for cement- and petroleum-based additive manufacturing. Natural fibers such as hemp and kenaf have been extensively employed as reinforcements in thermoplastic polymers.2–6 Nevertheless, the incorporation of natural fibers does not consistently enhance all mechanical properties of these biocomposites.2 On the contrary, fiber inclusion in earth-based mixtures allows for reduced shrinkage and improved mechanical strength.3 Specifically, 3D-printed earth, which utilizes raw clay-rich soils directly from the construction site, is critical for socially and environmentally just architectural design to reduce transportation, chemical treatments, excess manufacturing, and intermediary storage.7,8 Current applied design research on 3D-printed earth has been focusing on artifacts, structures, and elements using mix designs resembling a vernacular method known as cob.7 Cob is an earth mixture that contains clay-rich soil, with 2–3 wt% natural fibers. This mixture is produced in a plastic state and implemented wet to build monolithic load-bearing walls with or without formwork. The presence of straw in the cob was shown to impart a ductile failure mechanism, a quality that suggests appropriate behavior in seismic areas.9 However, due to its high mass capacity and low thermal resistivity, cob assemblies result in high mass assemblies as thick as 600 mm. While critically introducing a radically natural low-carbon mix design, digital earth using cob is still limited to massive, heavy, brittle, and thick structures that work in compression. Vernacularly, a lightweight and insulative counterpart to cob is a material system called light straw clay (also known as light clay, straw clay, slip straw, rammed straw, and leichtlehmbau in Germany). Light straw clay is an earth-fiber mixture that serves as an insulative infill mix with fiber (usually wheat straw) dampened with clay slurry (wet clay-rich soil), to be tamped within a structural frame.10,11 With an increased fiber content, light straw clay proposes a mix design characterized by a coupled insulation and mass capacity.12 Light straw clay has a mean heat transfer coefficient of 0.531 W/m2K for a 120-mm thick wall,13 making it a compatible insulation material that meets energy code requirements. In terms of its environmental impacts, light straw clay was shown to outperform all other earth—and conventional—assemblies over its cradle-to-grave life cycle.14 Light straw clay was shown to have the best embodied and operational carbon performance due to its low extractive constituent materials as well as the juxtaposition of thermal mass and thermal resistivity that reduces both heating and cooling energy demand. To advance the material design research of natural earth-fiber composites for 3D printing, this study aims to maximize fiber content in 3D printable earth-based mix designs, to achieve similar ratios as light straw clay, while testing the limits of maximum lightness, ductility and bending capacity, and geometrical craft. By optimizing mix designs for 3D-printed earth while maximizing fiber content, and thus, carbon storage4 and ductility, this project links, for the first time, natural 3D-printed vegetable fiber and earth artifacts for lightweight think construction. In the long term, this project hopes to push the use of recycled and recyclable bio-based materials for interior and temporary applications, an important aspect within the built environment that further requires low-carbon responses and subsequent end-of-life strategies. As recyclable natural materials can be re-plasticized when exposed to water, they also require proper design and interior applications to be protected from weatherization.15 Background on Natural Materials in Advanced Manufacturing Natural materials in architecture are defined as readily available, minimally processed, nontoxic, and community accessible.16 Due to their traditional nature and adaptability to various shapes and construction methods, natural building materials are often reminiscent of traditional architectural techniques. Natural materials include a range of naturally occurring substances but can be summarized into two base constituencies comprising geological products, such as earth, sand, larger aggregate, lime, and stone, and biological products, such as vegetation plants, plant-like protists such as algae, agro “waste,” and co-products within a circular economy logic. This range of substances can be combined into mix designs using construction methods that form techniques such as adobe, rammed earth, compressed earth blocks, cob, hempcrete, straw-bale construction, and light straw clay. Historically, natural earth-fiber composites can be found in almost every region of the world. Based on knowledge transmitted from one generation to the next, earth-fiber composites have been evolving over millennia into a range of techniques to respond to bioclimatic contexts and thermal and structural requirements. Traditional cob walls, for instance, can be found in temperate climates such as Devon England, heated by hearths within load-bearing assemblies using earth and straw placed in layers on stone plinths.17 In other contexts, the Hakka round rammed-earth houses in China provide a heavy thermal mass to regulate temperature and relative humidity.18 Fiber-based vernacular architecture that makes use of fast-growing and grass-like fiber-reinforced plants is found, for instance, in Peruvian Uros’s totora houses, bound with mud mortar,19 as well as thatched-roof houses in wetland areas, from South America to Africa and Europe.20 Light straw clay, which is the focus of this work, is a fiber-based mixture coated with clay-rich soil slurry, representing another example of fibrous traditional construction materials used as infill products and valued for its insulating and lightweight properties.21 As a radically sustainable material, light straw clay was shown to be crucial to maximizing carbon neutrality in the construction of thermally resistant infill assemblies.14 Despite their environmental and social benefits, natural materials are not without their limitations. Natural materials and methods, such as cob and light straw clay, are often slower and laborious. They were vernacularly termed the “slow process.”22 The main impediments to implementing earth materials in mainstream construction are indeed their lack of technical data and labor intensity throughout the construction process.23,24 Digital fabrication may, therefore, be a critical solution to increasing the implementation of natural materials, by reducing human labor intensity and the need for formwork, while also increasing speed and material economy.The state of the art of digital earth construction includes both material and machinery developments, from an applied and educational research scope. Applied projects include large-scale earth structures using traditional adobe recipes by studio Rael San Fratello and Muddy Robbots.1 Educational fronts include experimental earthen structural geometries, such as mud staircase, with a focus on residential construction, at The Institute for Advanced Architecture of Catalonia.25,26 Other applications, summarized in Figure 1, use adapted versions of cob mix designs. As a natural reinforcement, plant fibers have been used in 3D printing applications, mostly within a petroleum- or cement-based matrix. Most relevant to this study is LivMatS pavilion, which used flax fibers within oil-based epoxy (Hexion) and bio-based epoxy matrix (Greenpoxy), a bio-based resin that consists of 52% natural resources, robotically fabricated through coreless filament winding technology.27 Additional mixtures were developed for 3D printable natural fibers.26,28,29 For instance, Rech et al. developed printable mixtures with sawdust embedded within xanthan gum, which serves as a binder for cellulose insulation. Continuing this line of work, an ongoing material design research at the Columbia Natural Materials Lab has been focusing on a range of earth-fiber composites for additive manufacturing.31,32 This line of research hopes to integrate additional avenues for natural fibers in 3D printing, within an earth-based binding matrix, as illustrated in Figure 1. As part of this work, computational design methods, workflows, and tools were developed to produce architectural objects while employing ubiquitous resources, as a viable alternative to synthetic insulation, or as an amelioration of heavy-mass digital earth. Inspired by light straw clay as an alternative approach to the use of natural fibers, coated with clay slurry, this article describes an experimental apparatus and design demonstration development of 3D-printed earth-fiber tiles, from material mix recipes to printability, structural testing, and fabrication of the design demonstration. Materials and Methods The main goal of this study is to develop perforated lightweight and ductile earth-fiber tiles. As a mixed-method approach, the methodology combines experimental, computational, and fabrication apparatuses. As shown in Figure 2, the developed workflow includes the following steps: (1) optimizing mix designs for maximized fiber content and testing each mix design for extrudability and buildability; (2) studying geometrical patterns that allow minimum material within a panel element, specifically traditional Middle Eastern shading and fabric elements; (3) testing the structural capacity of the resulting geometries for bending, using the breaking load to assess the number of layers in the final printing application, and (4) fabricating the 3D-printed modular elements into a shape-giving form to generate a hung tessellating light installation that works in tension. As a final result, this study examines and exhibits the potential of the resulting earth-fiber tiles within 1014 Space for Ideas and Gallery in New York. Mix-design analysis The soil used in this study is sourced from a construction recycling quarry in Goshen, NY, and is of a dark-gray color with ∼10% clay content. The fiber used was wheat straw harvested locally from NY State, which comes in longer stalks (10–12 cm) and was then blended in the laboratory with a Vitamix blender to achieve a 1–3 mm fiber length. Additionally, for the purposes of pigmentation, 0.1 wt% of natural red clay (Newman’s red clay) was added, which is a low plasticity California iron-bearing fire-clay for pottery purposes (Fig. 3). Up to 5% food-grade polysaccharide additives (cellulose and alginate) were used to support material printability and increase viscosity and green strength. Mixtures were first assessed with a manual syringe and were then systematically tested using a Delta WASP 40100 Clay 3D printer (WASP, Italy) in terms of their extrudability and buildability with the objective of increasing the fiber content. Experiments were conducted with a nozzle of 6 mm, and pressure was kept constant at 0.4 MPa throughout the printing with a fixed layer height of 3 mm. Structural tests The goal of this step was to assess the number of printed layers required to achieve the minimum usable strength of the single module with minimum material content. Selected geometries were tested in 320 × 320 mm sample panels. Structural bending tests were used to assess the breaking load according to ASTM C67/C67M by applying a loading rate of 1.25 mm/min until failure. Three heights were assessed, each comprising a 3-mm layer height. Three specimens were tested for each tile type, for a total of nine elements. The breaking load ( 𝑝) was then calculated as the average according to the following equation (Eq 1): 𝑝=𝑃/𝑤 (Eq. 1) where 𝑃 is the maximum load (N) indicated by the testing machine, and w is the net width of the specimen (mm) at the plane of failure, namely 65 mm Results and Design Investigation Bespoke fibrous material—earth-fiber recipe results and reusability potential Starting with digital cob mixture proportions observed in the literature, preliminary mix designs were tested with 54 wt% of soil, 3 wt% of fibers, and 41 wt% of water. A cylinder with a 100 mm diameter, 50 mm height, and composed of 20 consecutive layers was printed for each admixture to determine the most suitable recipe in terms of printability. By keeping the soil content fixed, the fiber content was increased until it was no longer possible to extrude the paste from the 3D printer nozzle. Further detailed in previous work by the authors. As illustrated in Figure 3, the printed cylinder in light straw clay achieves an 80% weight decrease as opposed to the printed cob cylinder while assuring the extrudability and the buildability of the material. Importantly, the achieved material cob, light cob, and light straw clay mix designs were proven fully recyclable and reusable. Printing experimentation was fully reused by being immersed in water for 24 h with 79 wt% water, which is the percentage of water lost while drying. Once fully hydrated and “re-plasticized,” the material was manually mixed, similar to dough baking practices, to re-obtain a 3D-printable paste. The tile demonstration in this study highlights this circular capability by being printed and reused, several times throughout the process. Perforated lattice patterns following traditional weaving and Mashrabiya designs The pattern form-finding in this study aimed to optimize perforation and minimum material, for a 3D-printed line-deposited order. Path lines were investigated to ensure the structural integrity of the whole element, with two printed walls being braided as shown in Figure 4. A G-code generator script in Grasshopper was used to generate the final printing file. The geometric exploration began with an investigation into the symbolism and patterns in traditional fabric craft, followed by an investigation into shading patterns in North African and Mediterranean contexts. Traditional woven fabrics, such as the Noche de Berberisca dresses, exhibit weaved patterns that help construct fabric shape, as a reinforcement element. The investigation continued into the Arab shading element—the Mashrabiya typology found in cities with arid climates, such as Baghdad, Damascus, Cairo, Jeddah, and Tunis.33 Contemporary precedents show great interest in expanding Mashrabiya utilization beyond its traditional use. Some examples include Al Bahar Towers’ kinetic facades by Aedas Architects in Abu Dhabi34,35 and SDU Kolding Campus by Henning Larsen Architects in Denmark.36 Both precedents push Mashrabiya’s traditional use through computational design and day-lighting optimization.37 The pattern development was continued using Hilbert curves, a type of mathematical construct that generates intricate lattice patterns through a continuous line.38 Used previously by architects such as Wacław Szpakowski in single-line labyrinthine geometries,39 the Hilbert curves offered a unique approach for a “printable” line path architecture. The geometrical exploration developed and shown in Figure 4 concludes the rhythmic lines served as guidance for creating the final tiling pattern. A square grid of points was initially created as the basis for the lattice. The Hilbert curve was then constructed by dividing the square recursively into four smaller squares and connecting them in a specific sequence. The resulting curve visits every point in the grid precisely once, creating a continuous and nonintersecting path, as illustrated in Figure 4. The lattice was constructed by connecting the points in the grid along the path of the curve. By adjusting the spacing and thickness of the lattice elements, a wide range of patterns was created that are both visually intricate and structurally robust. Once the geometry for a single tile was determined, a modular system was developed by cataloging a grid system that could be repeated endlessly. To create a unified geometry, additional connection attachments between individual panels were designed to form a wet connection, where the modular panels were simply brought closer together to be dried uniformly. In this logic, first, a specific connector panel characterized by protrusions and insets combined within a singular tile module was designed to minimize the quantity of material printed. Several connector details were examined. The structural results, shown in Table 1, indicate that the addition of two layers increased the average p-value by 318% when compared with a geometry with just one printed layer. When analyzing the element’s weight, it is possible to observe that elements with three layers resulted in the addition of 45% of weight and almost improved by 199% the p-value with respect to specimens with two layers. Considering that this study aimed to minimize material in production while assuring the stability of the final result, it was decided that three layers would be used in the final printed demonstration. Furthermore, the slightly higher maximum breaking load achieved with additional layers compared with a single layer is presumably resulting from the formation of some intra-layer bonding. Independently of the printing pattern, decreasing the layer height typically leads to enhanced cohesion and tensile properties of the printed biocomposite.2 A layer height of 3 mm, which is half the nozzle size, was therefore chosen to increase the bonding strength between adjacent layers while ensuring a good load path transfer through the assembly. Future research is needed for earth-fiber mix designs to assess the impact of the fiber alignment along the printing paths and its effect on the bending strength. Production and final demonstration The digital fabrication steps followed three main production steps as shown in Figure 7: 3D printing of the tiles, assembly of the modular system, and installation upon drying. The printed modules were laid on top of a curved formwork made out of repurposed formwork to test the curvature potential of the material. Once the tiles are 3D printed, they are directly molded upon the reused formwork prior to the drying point while the material is still malleable. To obtain self-connected modules with the use of the wet connection, two subsequent modules were placed within 80 mm of distance, and a third module was located on top of them when the material was still wet and adjusted manually. By doing so, the final spatial tessellating geometry was bonded in a wet state, strengthening the connectivity between the tiles over a 36-h drying period. Once dried, the final 3D geometry was easily detachable from the formwork. The final spatial tessellating geometry detail is shown at the bottom of Figure 7, illustrating the passage of light through the artifact. The resulting demonstration, composed of solely natural materials in a regenerative perspective, allowed to parametrically control the graduation of light and shadow tracing filigree motives on the floors and walls and evoking echoes of traditional patterns. The developed materials and tectonic system were demonstrated in an interior light installation at 1014 Space for Ideas, a gallery space in East Side Manhattan. The light straw clay 3D-printed lightweight panels were designed to create a site-specific installation, placed within the gallery’s spiral 19th-century stairwell. Five tasseling panels were fabricated from a total of 39 tiles, printed over 8 hours, and hung using a transparent thread beaded using brass clips. Composed of a total of 3.5 kg straw fiber, these modular panels were connected in a wet state to form vertical curtain walls, allowing an interplay of light and shadow, as shown in Figure 8. From heavy mass to lightweight mesh, this installation demonstrates a design apparatus of spatial lines, in a line-based deposited order, to create lightweight 3D-printed earth-fiber assemblies Broader Implications and Future Wor The final installation demonstration provides a proof-of-concept prototype for novel earth-fiber mixtures. Additionally, this research proposes a workflow for investigating impactful, low-carbon, and lightweight fiber and earth-based materials in digital fabrication. With respect to the state of the art where 3D-printed earth materials have been used with 2% or less fiber content, this work develops an earth-fiber printing paste with 13 wt% of wheat straw content (49% natural fiber in volume). The results of the study show that the main parameters affecting the design, printability, and final behavior of the printed tiles relate to the length, content, and type of natural fiber used within the mix design. Overall, this study proved light straw clay’s printability and structural tensile capabilities, while respecting the vernacular lexicon and mix-design recipe. An important feature of this work is the full recyclability and reusability of the developed printing mixture. The printing experimentations shown in this article were fully reused by being immersed in water for 24 h with 79 wt% of water, “re-plasticized,” and then reprinted. The future of low-carbon earth-fiber composites in 3D printing filaments and lightweight construction materials critically suggests further applications, advancements, and demonstrations, from interior finish materials to hybrid mass-insulation wall assemblies. Future research should include comprehensive characterization and enumeration of the mechanical, thermal, and environmental performance of a range of plant fibers within “light clay” mix designs. This line of work is currently being expanded by the authors to an array of bast, stalk, leaf, and other vegetable co-product materials that can render the proposed construction methods even more resource-efficient and bioregional. Moreover, future developments should be carried out by keeping in mind the ultimate dimensions and shipment system for upscaled product development. To make the proposed 3D printing strategies globally applicable, the manual forms of making together with the use of local agriculture and construction geological co-products in contemporary industries is critically needed to enable the development of local digital craftsmanship. Ultimately, the combination of natural materials, both geological and biological, as well as the collaboration of human/machine fabrication process opens up important pathways to environmental, social, and cultural ancient just digital-craft future.

title: Sulfasalazine-Induced Hypoglycemia in a Patient with Type 2 Diabetes and End-Stage Renal Disease
html_text:
pmcAACE Clin Case RepAACE Clin Case Rep3582aaceccrAACE Clinical Case Reports2376-0605American Association of Clinical EndocrinologyPMC12335297PMC12335297.112335297123352974078684810.4158/ACCR-2018-0067S2376-0605(20)30129-21Case ReportsSulfasalazine-Induced Hypoglycemia in a Patient with Type 2 Diabetes and End-Stage Renal DiseaseStamatiadesGeorge A.MDstamatiadesg@gmail.com1*Echouffo-TcheuguiJustin B.MD, PhD1GarberJeffrey R.MD1,2From the Division of Endocrinology, Diabetes and Hypertension, Brigham and Women's Hospital, Harvard Medical School, Boston, MassachusettsHarvard Vanguard Medical Associates, Boston, Massachusetts.Address correspondence to Dr. George A. Stamatiades, Division of Endocrinology, Diabetes and Hypertension, Brigham and Women's Hospital, 221 Longwood Avenue, Boston, MA 02115. stamatiadesg@gmail.comNov-Dec20180812202046494706e493e496622018962018081220201008202511082025Copyright © 2018 AACE.2018Elsevier Inc.https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

Objective: To demonstrate important points regarding the possible hypoglycemic effects of sulfasalazine and suggest possible underlying mechanism(s) accounting for sulfasalazine-induced hypoglycemia.

Methods: We describe a case of reversible sulfasalazine-induced hypoglycemia, review the literature, and discuss a potential mechanism accounting for sulfasalazine-induced hypoglycemia.

Results: A 63-year-old man with Crohn disease treated with sulfasalazine and type 2 diabetes complicated by end-stage renal disease was admitted for treatment of persistent hypoglycemia. Insulinoma was initially suspected, but localization studies including endoscopic ultrasound were negative. This raised the possibility of sulfasalazine-induced hypoglycemia. Three days after sulfasalazine was stopped, he became normoglycemic. Hypoglycemia has not recurred since discontinuing sulfasalazine.

Conclusion: Clinicians should be aware of the potential hypoglycemic effect of sulfasalazine. Doses should be reduced in patients with impaired renal function, and it should be discontinued if otherwise unexplained hypoglycemia develops.

Abbreviations: CT = computed tomography; ESRD = end-stage renal disease

pmc-status-qastatus0pmc-status-liveyespmc-status-embargonopmc-status-releasedyespmc-prop-open-accessyespmc-prop-olfnopmc-prop-manuscriptnopmc-prop-legally-suppressednopmc-prop-has-pdfyespmc-prop-has-supplementnopmc-prop-pdf-onlynopmc-prop-suppress-copyrightnopmc-prop-is-real-versionnopmc-prop-is-scanned-articlenopmc-prop-preprintnopmc-prop-in-epmcyespmc-license-refCC BY-NC-NDINTRODUCTION

Drugs constitute the most common cause of hypoglycemia (1). Although insulin and insulin secretagogues, such as sulfonylureas, are the most common drugs that can cause hypoglycemia, several other pharmacologic agents that are used broadly in clinical practice can cause hypoglycemia (2). The most commonly reported drugs that are associated with hypoglycemia are quinolones, pentamidine, quinine, beta-blockers, angiotensin-converting enzyme agents, and insulin-like growth factor (3). Factors that predispose to drug-induced hypoglycemia are restricted food access, advanced age, liver disease, and impaired renal function (4).

An association between hypoglycemia and sulfasalazine has been previously identified, but the evidence in the literature is limited (5). Sulfasalazine, which is clinically useful in treating inflammatory bowel diseases, is a compound that is cleaved in vivo to 5-aminosalicylic acid and to the intestinal metabolite sulfapyridine, the latter accounting for most of the adverse effects of sulfasalazine (6). Sulfapyridine is 60% bioavailable after colonic absorption and 62% dialyzable (7). It is a sulfonamide, similar in structure to glyburide, an insulin secretagogue antidiabetic drug, which has greater hypoglycemic activity compared to the other commonly prescribed members of the sulfonylureas group, and its duration of action depends on renal excretion (8).

Herein, we present a case of reversible sulfasalazine-induced hypoglycemia in a man with Crohn disease treated with sulfasalazine and type 2 diabetes complicated by end-stage renal disease (ESRD), who was initially believed to have an insulinoma.

Case Report

A 63-year-old man with prior medical history of Crohn disease, hypothyroidism, coronary artery disease, and 30-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) complicated by 14 years of ESRD on hemodialysis, retinopathy (legally blind), autonomic neuropathy with gastroparesis, and significant peripheral neuropathy was admitted for treatment of recurrent hypoglycemia.

T2DM was diagnosed on routine screening 30 years prior to admission. Hemoglobin A1c levels ranged between 5 and 7% (31 and 53 mmol/mol) until 3 months prior to admission, when it dropped to 4.3% (23 mmol/mol). He was initially treated with oral agents and then transitioned to insulin therapy, which he had been taking for more than 20 years. Six months prior to admission, he was taking glargine 25 units and sliding-scale insulin aspart. Comorbidities and complications included coronary artery disease with ischemic cardiomyopathy, peripheral vascular disease, ESRD on hemodialysis (3 times a week), and diabetic retinopathy. He then frequently was hypoglycemic even after insulin was discontinued. His episodes of hypoglycemia were symptomatic, limited to tremor, nausea, and lightheadedness. The episodes of hypoglycemia were not related to the timing of his meals. His self-monitored blood glucose values ranged from 50 to 90 mg/dL. Administration of glucose tabs or food when tolerated led to the resolution of the symptoms. He had gallbladder resection 3 months prior to admission for chronic abdominal pain and gallstone pancreatitis. During the 2 months prior to admission due to abdominal discomfort, he ate less and lost approximately 30 pounds.

Investigation

He was initially evaluated at an outside hospital for recurrent hypoglycemia. His work-up showed insulin level 8 μIU/mL with elevated pro-insulin level 8.3 pmol/L (normal, <8.0 pmol/L) and elevated C-peptide of 5.2 ng/mL (normal, 0.8 to 3.5 ng/mL) with corresponding plasma glucose level of 48 mg/dL. Anti-insulin antibodies were negative, and a computed tomography (CT) scan of the pancreas was normal. He was referred to Brigham and Women's Hospital Surgical Oncology Department for evaluation for possible surgery for insulinoma. While hospitalized, he frequently had blood sugars below 55 mg/dL, which occurred with inappropriately normal insulin levels. A CT of the abdomen and pelvis for evaluating pancreatic lesions and an endoscopic ultrasound showed diffuse mild pancreatic parenchymal atrophy but did not show a mass, making the diagnosis of insulinoma very unlikely, since the overall sensitivity for combined CT and endoscopic sonography is reported to be quite high and, in some series, is 100% (9). The inappropriate insulin levels indicated he had insulin-mediated hypoglycemia. Therefore, non–insulin-mediated causes were not in the differential, limiting the differential diagnosis to causes of inappropriate insulin secretion besides insulinoma: factitious insulin use nesidioblastosis and drug-induced hypoglycemia. The elevated C-peptide levels ruled out exogenous insulin administration. Moreover, the fact that he did not have any visitors and he was blind made factitious insulin use or commonly prescribed oral hypoglycemic agents such as sulfonylureas as a possible cause of the hypoglycemia very unlikely. Nesidioblastosis could not be ruled out on the basis of negative imaging studies (10). However, in the absence of prior gastric bypass surgery, drug-induced hypoglycemia seemed more likely and was explored next. Hypercalcemia by increasing beta-cell responsiveness to sulfonylureas and hypothyroidism were excluded as possible etiologies or predisposing him to hypoglycemia, as calcium and thyroid-stimulating hormone levels were normal (8.9 mg/dL and 4.76 μIU/mL, respectively).

Drug-induced hypoglycemia via insulin secretion was thought to be the most probable cause of the persistent hypoglycemia. Among the medications he was taking (Table 1), the only one that has been reported to cause hypoglycemia, especially among people with diabetes on insulin or sulfonylurea, was sulfasalazine (5). Moreover, he was taking 3 g daily, whereas the daily dose for patients on dialysis should not exceed 500 mg (11).

Medications Prior to Admission

Table 1
Unlabelled Image

It was therefore possible that the hypoglycemic effect of sulfasalazine became manifest because it was taken in excessive amounts for someone with ESRD, who was malnourished, had inadequate caloric intake, and delayed gastric emptying (documented on nuclear medicine testing).

Outcome and Follow-Up

Sulfasalazine was stopped, and he ceased to have episodes of hypoglycemia in the hospital. His blood sulfonamide levels 48 hours after stopping the medication were above the upper limit of therapeutic range (9.2 μg/mL [reference, 5.0 μg/mL] and 13 μg/mL [reference, 5.0 μg/mL]), lending support to the hypothesis. The serum half-life of sulfasalazine is 6 or 14 hours, depending on the genetic phenotype (6). However, since sulfasalazine is renally excreted, the half-life in ESRD may be increased to 50 hours (12). After 72 hours, he became normoglycemic and was discharged off sulfasalazine and insulin (Fig. 1). Posthospitalization, the hypothesis of toxicity of sulfasalazine was verified. He underwent continuous glucose monitoring, which did not detect any hypoglycemia. Hypoglycemia has not recurred since discontinuing sulfasalazine.

Changes in blood glucose levels from admission (Day 1) to discharge (Day 11). *Sulfasalazine withdrawal (Day 2). Arrowheads boluses of intravenous glucose (g) that were delivered to the patient. +The therapeutic blood sulfonamide levels are up to 5 μg/mL.

Fig. 1

DISCUSSION

Our case report demonstrates important points regarding the possible hypoglycemic effects of sulfasalazine. Hypoglycemia induced by sulfasalazine is thought to be mainly via hyperinsulinemia, as suggested by the high levels of C-peptide, pro-insulin, and insulin. Although C-peptide and insulin levels can increase in renal disease due to compensatory hypersecretion of beta-cells, due to insulin resistance (13) and delayed clearance, this does not result in fasting or prolonged repetitive bouts of insulin-mediated hypoglycemia. Doses that exceed the amounts indicated in renal failure, the prolonged half-life of the medication due to impaired renal function, and malnutrition can exacerbate its hypoglycemic effect (4). It should be considered in the differential diagnosis of hypoglycemia and may have to be discontinued in order to reverse it, especially in patients with renal failure who are not on other blood sugar–lowering agents.

It is hypothesized that the hypoglycemic effect of sulfasalazine is due to the sulfapyridine component, a sulfonamide antibiotic. Biochemically, sulfapyridine is structurally similar to glyburide, a member of the sulfonylurea class of oral hypoglycemic agents (Fig. 2). Both are derivatives of the parent compound sulfanilamide (12). Hence, sulfonamides may cause hypoglycemia with the same mechanism of action as sulfonylureas, by binding to pancreatic beta-cells and stimulate insulin release (14,15). Although there are only 4 reported cases with sulfasalazine-induced hypoglycemia (5), there are 28 published cases of hypoglycemia associated with the use of cotrimoxazole (sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim), an antimicrobial drug, the sulfamethoxazole component of which is a derivative of sulfanilamide, as well (12,14). Another possible mechanism that can contribute further to the glucose-lowering effects of sulfasalazine is through the anti-inflammatory effect of the salicylate component of the drug. Studies have shown that salicylates can both inhibit hepatic glucose production and increase insulin sensitivity via inhibition of the kinase inhibitor of nuclear factor kappa-B kinase beta subunit, which plays a key role in tissue inflammation (16–18).

Same sulfanilamide structural group in sulfapyridine and glyburide. (A) Sulfapyridine; (B) Glyburide.

Fig. 2

It is noteworthy that most of the reported cases, including that reported here, were in patients with impaired renal function, indicating the contributing role of prolonged half-life in the hypoglycemic adverse effect of sulfonamides (12,19). Chronic renal failure also has clinically significant effects on drug metabolism and transport (20), suggesting additional reasons for the prolonged elevated levels of sulfasalazine after discontinuation.

CONCLUSION

In summary, clinicians should be aware of the potential hypoglycemic effects of treatment with sulfasalazine and should discontinue it if otherwise unexplained hypoglycemia develops. Sulfasalazine use in patients with impaired renal function must be undertaken with caution, ensuring that appropriate dose adjustments are made. Finally, further study is required to elucidate the cause of sulfasalazine-induced hypoglycemia.
